prompt.name: optimized-tests
prompt.content:
│ │ # Storybook Setup │ ## Project Info │ | Property | Value | │ |----------|-------| │ | Version | 10.4.0-alpha.10 | │ | Renderer | @storybook/react | │ | Framework | @storybook/react-vite | │ | Builder | @storybook/builder-vite | │ | Config Dir | `.storybook` | │ | CSF Format | CSF Factory | │ | Addons | @storybook/addon-onboarding, @storybook/addon-themes, │ @storybook/addon-docs, @storybook/addon-designs, │ @storybook/addon-vitest, @storybook/addon-a11y, │ storybook-addon-pseudo-states, @chromatic-com/storybook | │ Attention: The following instructions must be followed in order to │ successfully set up Storybook in this project. Do not skip steps or │ attempt to do them out of order. │ Your goal is to make Storybook fully functional in this project by │ analyzing the codebase, │ configuring the preview with the right decorators, and writing stories │ for some components. │ The end state should be a Storybook where any component — from a small │ button to a full page — can be added without story-specific │ workarounds. All necessary providers, CSS, browser state, and network │ mocks should live in the shared preview so that just rendering the │ component in the story is enough. │ After each created story, run Vitest to verify it renders. │ If the test fails, read the error, fix the issue, and re-run until it │ passes before moving on. │ - Copy real patterns from the codebase │ - Keep the app code unchanged │ - Put the default setup in `.storybook/preview.tsx` │ - Keep app mocking and runtime setup in `.storybook/preview.tsx`, not │ in the stories │ ### Storybook Documentation Reference │ Use the following references to look up Storybook APIs, concepts, or │ examples: │ - Full docs index: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/llms.txthttps://storybook.js.org/llms.txt]8;; │ - See code snippets only with codeOnly=true param e.g. │ ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=ts&codeOnly=truehttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=ts&codeOnly=true]8;; │ Key documentation pages for this task: │ - Writing stories: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories...]8;; │ - Decorators: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/decorators.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/deco...]8;; │ - Args: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/args.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/args.md?re...]8;; │ - Play functions: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/play-function.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/...]8;; │ - Vitest integration: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-tests/vitest-plugin.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-test...]8;; │ Fetch these URLs directly when you need guidance on Storybook APIs or │ patterns. │ ### Step 1: Analyze the codebase │ Read enough of the app to understand the full runtime environment │ before writing any stories. │ Do not stop at `main.tsx` or `App.tsx`. │ Follow imports into providers, pages, hooks, and shared components │ until you know: │ - which providers exist │ - which CSS files are injected │ - which queries fetch data │ - which browser-state reads happen │ - which portals and portal roots exist │ - which pages and components show the real usage patterns │ Example of what to copy: │ ```tsx │ // src/main.tsx │ import "./index.css"; │ import App from "./App"; │ import { SessionProvider } from "./contexts/SessionContext"; │ createRoot(document.getElementById("root")!).render( │ │ │ , │ ); │ ``` │ That means Storybook should copy: │ - the `index.css` import │ - the `SessionProvider` │ - the same provider order │ Example of tracing the app deeper: │ ```tsx │ // src/App.tsx │ function App() { │ const { products, loadMoreProducts } = useProducts(); │ const { currentUser, signOut } = useSession(); │ // ... │ } │ ``` │ ```ts │ // src/hooks/useProducts.ts │ const response = await fetch(apiBaseUrl + "/products?page=1"); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // src/hooks/useTheme.ts │ const savedTheme = localStorage.getItem("theme"); │ ``` │ That means the default Storybook setup should discover and prepare: │ - provider state │ - MSW handlers for queries │ - browser-state values that are actually read during render │ ### Step 2: Build one default app environment in preview │ Set up Storybook once so most stories work without story-specific │ setup. │ Start with the smallest faithful environment: │ - the real provider tree │ - the real root CSS │ - seeded browser state if the app reads it during render │ - MSW for network/data queries │ It is fine to seed browser state such as `localStorage`, │ `sessionStorage`, and cookies when the app reads them during render. │ Seed only the specific app-owned keys and values you need. │ Do not clear all `localStorage`, `sessionStorage`, or cookies, and do │ not reset Storybook's own state. │ Do not mock or redefine the browser runtime itself. │ The stories run in Vitest browser mode, so the real browser │ environment should already exist. │ ```tsx │ // .storybook/preview.tsx │ import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles │ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; │ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; │ import { SessionProvider } from '../src/contexts/SessionContext'; │ export default definePreview({ │ decorators: [ │ (Story) => ( │ │ │ │ ), │ ], │ async beforeEach() { │ localStorage.setItem('theme', 'dark'); │ localStorage.setItem('sidebar:open', 'true'); │ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ Use this same idea for: │ - providers │ - root CSS │ - browser state │ - dates, and if the app logic depends on them during render then │ always use `mockdate` │ Example with the `mockdate` package: │ ```tsx │ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; │ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; │ export default definePreview({ │ async beforeEach() { │ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ ### Step 3: Support portals with preview-body.html │ If the app uses portals, copy that setup into Storybook too. │ Look for patterns like: │ - `createPortal(...)` │ - modal, dialog, drawer, popover, tooltip, toast, or dropdown portal │ components │ - hard-coded roots such as `#portal-root`, `#modal-root`, │ `#drawer-root`, or `#toast-root` │ Example of what to copy: │ ```tsx │ // real component │ return createPortal(, │ document.getElementById("portal-root")!); │ ``` │ That means Storybook should create the same portal root in │ `.storybook/preview-body.html`: │ ```html │ │
│ ``` │ If the app uses multiple portal roots, create all of them there: │ ```html │ │
│
│
│ ``` │ If a library portals directly to `document.body`, do not add extra │ roots for it. │ Make sure the copied page shell, CSS, and layout still allow overlays, │ fixed positioning, and z-index stacking to render correctly. │ ### Step 4: Mock side effects globally │ All network/data queries should be handled by the default Storybook │ environment. │ - Always use `msw-storybook-addon` for query mocking. │ - If you introduce MSW, run `npx msw init ./public --save` to create │ the worker file. │ - Make sure Storybook serves `./public` as a static dir so │ `mockServiceWorker.js` is available. │ - Do not mock `fetch` directly. │ - Network/data queries should return deterministic mock data. │ - If you need to change dependencies, first check the lockfile and use │ that package manager for the change. │ Example of copying a real fetch pattern into shared handlers: │ ```ts │ // real app hook │ const response = await fetch( │ apiBaseUrl + │ "/products?" + │ new URLSearchParams({ │ page: "1", │ sort: "featured", │ }), │ ); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/msw-handlers.ts │ import { http, HttpResponse } from "msw"; │ export const mswHandlers = { │ products: [ │ http.get("]8;;https://api.example.com/products",https://api.example.com/products",]8;; │ () => │ HttpResponse.json({ │ items: [ │ { │ id: "product-1", │ name: "Example product", │ description: "Mock product description", │ imageUrl: │ "]8;;https://images.example.com/product.jpg",https://images.example.com/product.jpg",]8;; │ price: 42, │ }, │ ], │ }), │ ), │ ], │ }; │ ``` │ ```tsx │ // .storybook/preview.tsx │ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; │ import { initialize, mswLoader } from 'msw-storybook-addon'; │ import { mswHandlers } from './msw-handlers'; │ initialize({ │ onUnhandledRequest: 'bypass', │ }); │ export default definePreview({ │ loaders: [mswLoader], │ parameters: { │ msw: { │ handlers: mswHandlers, │ }, │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/main.ts │ import type { StorybookConfig } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ const config: StorybookConfig = { │ staticDirs: ["../public"], │ }; │ export default config; │ ``` │ Keep these mocks global. │ Do not put fetch mocks in individual stories. │ Only add handlers for requests that the shared preview setup or the │ stories actually use. │ Do not add catch-all handlers that can hide unrelated failures. │ If the defaults are not enough, improve the shared default setup │ instead. │ Seed browser state when needed, but do not mock `window`, `document`, │ `navigator`, observers, or similar runtime APIs. │ The only exception is `mockdate` when date-based rendering exists. │ ### Step 5: Write stories │ Try to find around 10 good candidate components for story files. │ Write colocated stories for top-level components, from low-level │ reusable components up to page components. │ Write up to 10 story files, or fewer only if the codebase clearly has │ fewer meaningful targets. │ The stories should use JSX copied from real usage patterns in: │ - pages │ - app shells │ - routes │ - tests │ - existing feature code │ As a rule of thumb, each story file should have around 3 story exports │ when the component or page has enough meaningful states. │ It can have more when the real usage supports it, up to 10 story │ exports in one file. │ Always show all imports explicitly in story and preview files. │ Do not rely on omitted or implied imports in examples or generated │ code. │ #### Story tags │ Every story meta must include the `ai-generated` tag to identify │ AI-created stories: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { SomeComponent } from './SomeComponent'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const Default = meta.story({ │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ If a story could not be fully fixed after the self-healing loop (the │ test still fails │ or the rendering is incomplete), add the `needs-work` tag alongside │ `ai-generated`: │ ```ts │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated', 'needs-work'], │ }); │ ``` │ #### Args vs render │ For simple components where props drive the state, prefer `args` │ stories — no `render` function needed: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { Button } from './Button'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: Button, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const Primary = meta.story({ │ args: { │ variant: 'primary', │ children: 'Save', │ }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i │ })).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }); │ export const Disabled = meta.story({ │ args: { │ variant: 'primary', │ disabled: true, │ children: 'Save', │ }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeDisabled(); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ Use `render` when the story needs composition — wrapping the component │ in layout, combining multiple components, or passing children as JSX: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { Button } from './Button'; │ import { Card } from './Card'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: Button, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const InsideCard = meta.story({ │ render: () => ( │ │ │ │ ), │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i │ })).toBeVisible(); │ await userEvent.click(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i })); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ Keep app mocking and runtime setup in preview, not in the stories. │ Do not build large story-specific harnesses. │ Do not write story files for subcomponents, hooks, contexts, or │ helpers. │ Do not create new application components. │ Do not add a custom `title`. │ Do not stop after only a few easy targets if the codebase has more │ meaningful components or pages available. │ ### Step 6: Write a play function for every story │ Every named story export must have a `play` function. │ The `play` function is not optional, even for simple stories. │ The purpose of the `play` function is to prove that the story actually │ works in the copied Storybook environment: │ - the story renders something real and non-empty │ - the decorators provide the needed context │ - the CSS is applied well enough for the intended state to be visible │ - the MSW mocks or seeded browser state are actually being used │ - important interactions, async loading states, and portals behave │ correctly │ Use `play` functions to verify behavior, not just to click around. │ A story without assertions is incomplete. │ Use tools from `storybook/test` such as: │ - `expect` │ - `waitFor` │ Prefer `canvas` and `userEvent` from the `play` context. │ Do not destructure `canvasElement` just to create `const canvas = │ within(canvasElement)`. │ Do not import `userEvent` from `storybook/test`; use `userEvent` from │ the `play` context instead. │ Only use `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when you need to query outside │ the canvas, such as for portals. │ Example: │ ```tsx │ import type { StoryObj } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ export const FilledForm: Story = { │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ const emailInput = canvas.getByLabelText("email", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(emailInput, "example-email@email.com", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const passwordInput = canvas.getByLabelText("password", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(passwordInput, "ExamplePassword", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const submitButton = canvas.getByRole("button"); │ await userEvent.click(submitButton); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ The assertions should match the real pattern you copied: │ - for provider-backed stories, assert the provider-dependent UI │ appears correctly │ - for mocked-data stories, wait for the mocked data to appear and │ assert on it │ - for CSS-sensitive states, assert on visibility, text layout, │ class-driven states, or meaningful computed styles │ - for routing or navigation stories, assert the routed state or │ navigation outcome │ - for portal stories, query from `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when │ the UI renders outside the canvas │ Examples of useful checks: │ - a themed button has the expected label and is visibly enabled or │ disabled │ - a modal opened through a decorator or provider is visible in the │ portal root │ - mocked API data appears in the page instead of a loading spinner │ forever │ - a selected tab actually shows the selected panel │ - a toast, alert, or badge has the expected accessible text and visual │ state │ - a CSS class or computed style confirms the real state that matters │ ### Step 7: Prove CSS is loaded in exactly one story named `CssCheck` │ In exactly one story, named `CssCheck`, assert a component-specific │ computed style. `toBeVisible` passes on an unstyled component; a │ concrete style value proves the shared preview loaded the app's CSS. │ Pick a visually distinctive component, read a styling value from its │ source, and assert it with `getComputedStyle`: │ ```tsx │ export const CssCheck: Story = { │ args: { children: "Submit" }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ const button = canvas.getByRole("button", { name: /submit/i }); │ // PrimaryButton uses bg-blue-600 — fails if Tailwind / global CSS did │ not load. │ await expect(getComputedStyle(button).backgroundColor).toBe("rgb(37, │ 99, 235)"); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ ### Step 8: Cover the patterns you found │ Write stories for the real patterns in the codebase, for example: │ - a low-level reusable component in real JSX usage │ - a provider-backed component │ - a browser-state-backed component │ - a fetched-data component │ - a real page component │ Use `App.tsx` to inspect the real provider tree and usage patterns, │ but do not make a story for `App` when the codebase has actual page │ components. │ Example page story: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { ProductPage } from './ProductPage'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: ProductPage, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const Default = meta.story({ │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect( │ canvas.getByRole('heading', { name: /products/i }), │ ).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ ### Step 9: Verify both rendering and types │ As you work, verify the stories with Vitest: │ ```bash │ npx vitest --project storybook │ ``` │ Also verify types so you catch missing required props, broken imports, │ and preview typing issues. Run the same TypeScript command the project │ itself uses. │ ```bash │ │ ``` │ After verification passes, review every changed file and remove │ anything that is not needed for the final solution, especially debug │ fixes, overly broad mocks, unnecessary dependencies, and eval │ artifacts. │ Keep iterating until: │ - every story you wrote passes │ - every story you wrote has a meaningful passing `play` function │ - the changed stories and preview setup pass the project's real │ TypeScript check │ - the rendered output looks sensible │ - the default global mocked environment is strong enough that stories │ do not need manual fetch overrides │ - stories no longer fail because the shared preview setup and story │ JSX are fixed │ - all passing stories have `tags: ['ai-generated']` in their meta │ - any stories that still need work have `tags: ['ai-generated', │ 'needs-work']` in their meta

content.thinking: Let me start by analyzing the codebase to understand the project structure, providers, CSS, data fetching patterns, and components.

input.prompt:
Thoroughly explore this codebase to understand:
1. Project structure - list all directories and key files
2. Entry point (main.tsx/index.tsx) - what providers, CSS imports, and setup exists
3. App.tsx - what's the provider tree, routing, layout
4. All context/provider files
5. All hooks that fetch data (look for fetch, axios, useQuery, etc.)
6. All browser state reads (localStorage, sessionStorage, cookies)
7. Portal usage (createPortal, modal roots, etc.)
8. All page-level components
9. All reusable UI components
10. Package manager (check for lock files)
11. TypeScript config
12. Existing .storybook config (main.ts, preview.ts, etc.)
13. Any existing stories
14. CSS framework used (Tailwind, CSS modules, styled-components, etc.)
15. Router used (react-router, etc.)

Be very thorough - read the actual file contents of main.tsx, App.tsx, all providers, all hooks, and key components. I need to understand every aspect of the runtime environment.

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have all the information I need. Let me compile the comprehensive exploration report:

## Comprehensive Codebase Exploration Report

### 1. Project Structure

**Root Directory:**
- /Users/yannbraga/open-source/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-28T22-14-57Z-715c7d59/project

**Key Directories:**
```
src/
├── airtable/                 (Airtable database integration)
├── assets/                   (Images and SVG icons)
│   ├── images/              (PNG icons and product images)
│   └── socials/             (LinkedIn, GitHub, portfolio SVGs)
├── components/              (Reusable UI components)
│   ├── cta/
│   ├── footer/
│   ├── navigation/
│   ├── team/
│   ├── JobsForm.jsx
│   ├── Newsletter.jsx
│   └── NotFound.jsx
├── core-ui/                 (index.css with Tailwind + custom CSS vars)
├── data/                    (Static data files)
├── routes/                  (Page-level components organized by route)
│   ├── about/
│   ├── blog/
│   ├── blog-post/
│   ├── contact/
│   ├── faq/
│   ├── jobs/
│   ├── landing/
│   ├── pricing/
│   ├── privacy/
│   ├── refunds/
│   ├── sign-in/
│   ├── sign-up/
│   ├── terms/
│   └── user-profile/
├── stories/                 (Storybook stories + example components)
├── index.jsx               (Entry point)
└── App.jsx                 (Main app component with routing)

.storybook/
├── main.ts                 (Storybook configuration)
├── preview.tsx             (Storybook preview settings)
└── eval-support/           (Evaluation-specific support files)

api/                        (Backend API folder - Express server)
```

### 2. Entry Point (index.jsx)

**File:** `/src/index.jsx`

Simple entry point that:
- Uses React 18 `createRoot` to mount to `#root` DOM element
- Imports `App` component and global CSS (`core-ui/index.css`)
- No providers or middleware

```javascript
import { createRoot } from "react-dom/client";
import App from "./App";
import "./core-ui/index.css";

const container = document.getElementById("root");
const root = createRoot(container);
root.render(<App />);
```

**HTML Entry:** `/index.html`
- Bootstrap script for Google reCAPTCHA API
- Meta tags for SEO
- Single root div with id="root"
- Loads `/src/index.jsx` as module

### 3. App.jsx - Provider Tree and Routing

**File:** `/src/App.jsx`

**No traditional providers used** - State is managed locally in App component.

**Provider Tree (Simple):**
```
App
├── Router (react-router-dom BrowserRouter)
├── Navigation (stateless, receives props)
├── Routes
│   ├── Route / → Landing
│   ├── Route /contact → Contact
│   ├── Route /pricing → Pricing
│   ├── Route /faq → Faq
│   ├── Route /blog → Blog
│   ├── Route /blog/:name → BlogPost
│   ├── Route /about → About
│   ├── Route /terms → Terms
│   ├── Route /refunds → Refunds
│   ├── Route /privacy → Privacy
│   ├── Route /jobs → Jobs
│   ├── Route /sign-in → SignIn or Profile (conditional on loggedIn)
│   ├── Route /sign-up → SignUp or Profile (conditional on loggedIn)
│   ├── Route /profile → Profile (protected, conditional on loggedIn)
│   └── Route * → NotFound
├── Footer (stateless)
└── Router end
```

**State Management (Local to App):**
- `user` - current logged-in user object (or null)
- `loggedIn` - boolean for auth state
- `triggeredLogout` - boolean flag for logout trigger

**Key App Functions:**
- `retrieveDatabase(email, password)` - fetch user from Airtable server
- `userEmailExists(email)` - check if email is registered
- `registerUser(data)` - create new user in Airtable
- `updateUser(userId, formValue)` - update user data
- `deleteUser(userId)` - delete user from Airtable

**CSS Imports:**
- `./core-ui/index.css` (imported in index.jsx, not App.jsx)

### 4. Context/Provider Files

**NO Context or Provider files exist in this project.**

State is managed locally in App.jsx and passed as props to child components. The pattern is prop-drilling for authentication state (`loggedIn`, `user`, setters).

### 5. All Hooks That Fetch Data

**Fetch/API Calls Found:**

1. **App.jsx:**
   - `retrieveDatabase()` - Fetches from `VITE_AIRTABLE_SERVER_URL` to get user records
   - Uses native `fetch()` API
   - Calls Airtable SDK: `edgyBase("users").create()`, `edgyBase("users").destroy()`

2. **SignUpForm.jsx:**
   - `verifyCaptcha()` - Fetches from `VITE_SERVER_URL` to verify ReCAPTCHA
   - `registerUser()` - Called from App, creates user via Airtable SDK

3. **SignInForm.jsx:**
   - `verifyCaptcha()` - Fetches from `VITE_SERVER_URL` to verify ReCAPTCHA
   - `retrieveDatabase()` - Fetches user data

4. **ContactForm.jsx:**
   - `verifyCaptcha()` - Fetches from `VITE_SERVER_URL`

5. **Airtable Integration:**
   - `/src/airtable/airtable.js` - Airtable SDK configuration
   - Creates base instance: `new Airtable({ apiKey }).base(baseId)`
   - Uses environment variables: `VITE_AIRTABLE_KEY`, `VITE_AIRTABLE_ID`, `VITE_AIRTABLE_SERVER_URL`, `VITE_SERVER_URL`

**No React Query, SWR, or advanced data fetching patterns used.**

### 6. Browser State Reads

**SessionStorage Usage (in App.jsx):**
- `sessionStorage.getItem("loggedIn")` - Check if user is logged in
- `sessionStorage.getItem("user")` - Retrieve serialized user object
- `sessionStorage.setItem("loggedIn", "true")` - Store login state
- `sessionStorage.setItem("user", JSON.stringify(userData))` - Store user data
- `sessionStorage.removeItem("loggedIn")` - Clear on logout
- `sessionStorage.removeItem("user")` - Clear on logout

**LocalStorage:** Not used

**Cookies:** Not used (Google reCAPTCHA manages its own cookies)

### 7. Portal Usage

**No React portals found.** No `ReactDOM.createPortal()` or modal roots detected.

### 8. All Page-Level Components

Located in `/src/routes/`:

1. **landing/Landing.jsx** - Home page (composition of multiple sections)
   - Imports: Hero, Partners, Feature, Cta, Stats, Pricing, Testimonials, Team, Comparison, Newsletter
   - Receives: `loggedIn` prop

2. **contact/Contact.jsx** - Contact page with form
   - Imports: ContactForm, CareerCta, ContactFormSection, ContactImg
   - No props required

3. **pricing/Pricing.jsx** - Pricing page
   - Imports: PricingComparisonTable, PricingFaq
   - Receives: `loggedIn` prop

4. **faq/Faq.jsx** - FAQ page
   - Imports: FaqList

5. **blog/Blog.jsx** - Blog listing page
   - Imports: BlogGrid

6. **blog-post/BlogPost.jsx** - Individual blog post
   - Uses route parameter `:name`

7. **about/About.jsx** - About page
   - Imports: AboutOne, AboutTwo, AboutThree

8. **terms/Terms.jsx** - Terms of service
   - Static content

9. **refunds/Refunds.jsx** - Refund policy
   - Static content

10. **privacy/Privacy.jsx** - Privacy policy
    - Static content

11. **jobs/Jobs.jsx** - Jobs listing page
    - Imports: JobsListing

12. **sign-in/SignIn.jsx** - Sign in page
    - Imports: SignInForm
    - Receives: `retrieveDatabase`, `user`, `setLoggedIn` props

13. **sign-up/SignUp.jsx** - Sign up page
    - Imports: SignUpForm
    - Receives: `registerUser` prop

14. **user-profile/Profile.jsx** - User profile (protected route)
    - Receives: `user`, `updateUser`, `deleteUser`, `setLoggedIn`, `setTriggeredLogout` props

15. **NotFound.jsx** - 404 page

### 9. All Reusable UI Components

Located in `/src/components/`:

1. **navigation/Navigation.jsx**
   - Header with logo, navigation links, auth buttons
   - Props: `loggedIn`, `setLoggedIn`, `setTriggeredLogout`
   - Uses Tailwind classes

2. **footer/Footer.jsx**
   - Footer with navigation links and social icons
   - Static content with maps over navigation objects
   - No props

3. **cta/Cta.jsx**
   - Call-to-action section
   - Props: `loggedIn`
   - Link to sign-up or profile based on auth state

4. **team/Team.jsx**
   - Team display component

5. **Newsletter.jsx**
   - Email subscription form
   - Uses state: `formValue`, `formError`, `submit`, `loading`
   - Form validation

6. **JobsForm.jsx**
   - Job application form
   - Uses state for form management

7. **stories/Button.jsx** - Storybook example component
8. **stories/Header.jsx** - Storybook example component
9. **stories/Page.jsx** - Storybook example component

### 10. Package Manager

**Package Manager:** npm (confirmed by presence of `package-lock.json`)

**Lock File:** `/package-lock.json` exists

**Package.json Key Info:**
- `"type": "module"` - ES modules enabled
- `"private": true` - Private package

### 11. TypeScript Config

**No TypeScript config found** in project root. Project uses JSX with JavaScript (.jsx/.js files), not TypeScript.

Storybook uses TypeScript for config files (`main.ts`, `preview.tsx`) but the app code is pure JavaScript/JSX.

### 12. Existing Storybook Config

**Main Config:** `//.storybook/main.ts`
```typescript
- stories pattern: '../src/**/*.mdx', '../src/**/*.stories.@(js|jsx|mjs|ts|tsx)', './eval-support/*.mdx'
- addons: @chromatic-com/storybook, @storybook/addon-vitest, @storybook/addon-a11y, @storybook/addon-docs, @storybook/addon-onboarding
- framework: @storybook/react-vite
```

**Preview Config:** `//.storybook/preview.tsx`
```typescript
- controls matchers for color and date
- a11y addon with test: 'todo'
```

**Vite Integration:** Uses `@storybook/react-vite` as framework

**Testing:** Configured with `@storybook/addon-vitest`, Vitest, and Playwright for browser testing

### 13. Existing Stories

Located in `/src/stories/`:

1. **Button.stories.js**
   - Button component with Primary, Secondary, Large, Small variants
   - Uses `fn()` for click handlers
   - Autodocs enabled

2. **Header.stories.js**
   - Header component with LoggedIn and LoggedOut variants
   - Props: user, onLogin, onLogout, onCreateAccount

3. **Page.stories.js**
   - Page component with LoggedOut and LoggedIn variants
   - LoggedIn variant includes interaction test (play function)

4. **Configure.mdx**
   - Documentation page about configuring Storybook

### 14. CSS Framework

**Tailwind CSS v4 with @tailwindcss/vite plugin**

**CSS Setup:**
- `/src/core-ui/index.css` - Main CSS file
  - Imports `@import "tailwindcss";`
  - Defines CSS custom properties:
    - `--primary-font-color: rgb(147, 157, 250)` (purple)
    - `--primary-font-color-hover: rgb(82, 98, 245)` (darker purple)
  - Base styles for body and code

**Tailwind Config:** `/tailwind.config.js`
```javascript
content: ["./index.html", "./src/**/*.{js,jsx,ts,tsx}"]
theme: { extend: {} }
plugins: []
```

**No CSS modules or styled-components used** - Pure Tailwind + custom CSS variables

**CSS Classes Example:**
- `bg-gray-900`, `text-white`, `flex`, `items-center`, etc.

### 15. Router

**Router:** react-router-dom v7.7.0

**Type:** BrowserRouter with declarative Routes

**Configuration:**
- No basename specified
- Standard React Router setup with `<Routes>` and `<Route>` components
- Conditional routing based on `loggedIn` state for protected routes
- useNavigate hook used for programmatic navigation
- Link components throughout for navigation

**Navigation Features:**
- Reset scroll on navigation: `window.scrollTo(0, 0)`
- Route parameters supported: `/blog/:name`
- Catch-all wildcard route for 404s: `<Route path="*" element={<NotFound />} />`

---

## Summary

This is a **Next.js/React marketing and SaaS dashboard website** for "Edgy" - an Instagram growth agency. Key characteristics:

- **No Context API or Provider pattern** - Simple prop drilling for auth state
- **Minimal data fetching** - Basic fetch() calls to Airtable backend
- **SessionStorage-based auth** - Session management via browser storage
- **Tailwind CSS + custom CSS variables** - Styling approach
- **react-router-dom v7** - Client-side routing
- **Vite + React** - Build and dev tooling
- **Storybook 0.0.0 (PR build)** - Component documentation
- **Airtable for database** - Backend is custom Node/Express server with Airtable integration
- **No portals** - Simple DOM structure
- **62 JS/JSX files total** - Modest codebase size
- **ReCAPTCHA integration** - Form verification on sign-up and sign-in

All state management is prop-based, authentication is sessionStorage-based, and there are no complex provider trees or custom hooks for data fetching.

Perfect! Now I have all the information I need. Let me compile the comprehensive exploration report:

## Comprehensive Codebase Exploration Report

### 1. Project Structure

**Root Directory:**
- /Users/yannbraga/open-source/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-28T22-14-57Z-715c7d59/project

**Key Directories:**
```
src/
├── airtable/                 (Airtable database integration)
├── assets/                   (Images and SVG icons)
│   ├── images/              (PNG icons and product images)
│   └── socials/             (LinkedIn, GitHub, portfolio SVGs)
├── components/              (Reusable UI components)
│   ├── cta/
│   ├── footer/
│   ├── navigation/
│   ├── team/
│   ├── JobsForm.jsx
│   ├── Newsletter.jsx
│   └── NotFound.jsx
├── core-ui/                 (index.css with Tailwind + custom CSS vars)
├── data/                    (Static data files)
├── routes/                  (Page-level components organized by route)
│   ├── about/
│   ├── blog/
│   ├── blog-post/
│   ├── contact/
│   ├── faq/
│   ├── jobs/
│   ├── landing/
│   ├── pricing/
│   ├── privacy/
│   ├── refunds/
│   ├── sign-in/
│   ├── sign-up/
│   ├── terms/
│   └── user-profile/
├── stories/                 (Storybook stories + example components)
├── index.jsx               (Entry point)
└── App.jsx                 (Main app component with routing)

.storybook/
├── main.ts                 (Storybook configuration)
├── preview.tsx             (Storybook preview settings)
└── eval-support/           (Evaluation-specific support files)

api/                        (Backend API folder - Express server)
```

### 2. Entry Point (index.jsx)

**File:** `/src/index.jsx`

Simple entry point that:
- Uses React 18 `createRoot` to mount to `#root` DOM element
- Imports `App` component and global CSS (`core-ui/index.css`)
- No providers or middleware

```javascript
import { createRoot } from "react-dom/client";
import App from "./App";
import "./core-ui/index.css";

const container = document.getElementById("root");
const root = createRoot(container);
root.render(<App />);
```

**HTML Entry:** `/index.html`
- Bootstrap script for Google reCAPTCHA API
- Meta tags for SEO
- Single root div with id="root"
- Loads `/src/index.jsx` as module

### 3. App.jsx - Provider Tree and Routing

**File:** `/src/App.jsx`

**No traditional providers used** - State is managed locally in App component.

**Provider Tree (Simple):**
```
App
├── Router (react-router-dom BrowserRouter)
├── Navigation (stateless, receives props)
├── Routes
│   ├── Route / → Landing
│   ├── Route /contact → Contact
│   ├── Route /pricing → Pricing
│   ├── Route /faq → Faq
│   ├── Route /blog → Blog
│   ├── Route /blog/:name → BlogPost
│   ├── Route /about → About
│   ├── Route /terms → Terms
│   ├── Route /refunds → Refunds
│   ├── Route /privacy → Privacy
│   ├── Route /jobs → Jobs
│   ├── Route /sign-in → SignIn or Profile (conditional on loggedIn)
│   ├── Route /sign-up → SignUp or Profile (conditional on loggedIn)
│   ├── Route /profile → Profile (protected, conditional on loggedIn)
│   └── Route * → NotFound
├── Footer (stateless)
└── Router end
```

**State Management (Local to App):**
- `user` - current logged-in user object (or null)
- `loggedIn` - boolean for auth state
- `triggeredLogout` - boolean flag for logout trigger

**Key App Functions:**
- `retrieveDatabase(email, password)` - fetch user from Airtable server
- `userEmailExists(email)` - check if email is registered
- `registerUser(data)` - create new user in Airtable
- `updateUser(userId, formValue)` - update user data
- `deleteUser(userId)` - delete user from Airtable

**CSS Imports:**
- `./core-ui/index.css` (imported in index.jsx, not App.jsx)

### 4. Context/Provider Files

**NO Context or Provider files exist in this project.**

State is managed locally in App.jsx and passed as props to child components. The pattern is prop-drilling for authentication state (`loggedIn`, `user`, setters).

### 5. All Hooks That Fetch Data

**Fetch/API Calls Found:**

1. **App.jsx:**
   - `retrieveDatabase()` - Fetches from `VITE_AIRTABLE_SERVER_URL` to get user records
   - Uses native `fetch()` API
   - Calls Airtable SDK: `edgyBase("users").create()`, `edgyBase("users").destroy()`

2. **SignUpForm.jsx:**
   - `verifyCaptcha()` - Fetches from `VITE_SERVER_URL` to verify ReCAPTCHA
   - `registerUser()` - Called from App, creates user via Airtable SDK

3. **SignInForm.jsx:**
   - `verifyCaptcha()` - Fetches from `VITE_SERVER_URL` to verify ReCAPTCHA
   - `retrieveDatabase()` - Fetches user data

4. **ContactForm.jsx:**
   - `verifyCaptcha()` - Fetches from `VITE_SERVER_URL`

5. **Airtable Integration:**
   - `/src/airtable/airtable.js` - Airtable SDK configuration
   - Creates base instance: `new Airtable({ apiKey }).base(baseId)`
   - Uses environment variables: `VITE_AIRTABLE_KEY`, `VITE_AIRTABLE_ID`, `VITE_AIRTABLE_SERVER_URL`, `VITE_SERVER_URL`

**No React Query, SWR, or advanced data fetching patterns used.**

### 6. Browser State Reads

**SessionStorage Usage (in App.jsx):**
- `sessionStorage.getItem("loggedIn")` - Check if user is logged in
- `sessionStorage.getItem("user")` - Retrieve serialized user object
- `sessionStorage.setItem("loggedIn", "true")` - Store login state
- `sessionStorage.setItem("user", JSON.stringify(userData))` - Store user data
- `sessionStorage.removeItem("loggedIn")` - Clear on logout
- `sessionStorage.removeItem("user")` - Clear on logout

**LocalStorage:** Not used

**Cookies:** Not used (Google reCAPTCHA manages its own cookies)

### 7. Portal Usage

**No React portals found.** No `ReactDOM.createPortal()` or modal roots detected.

### 8. All Page-Level Components

Located in `/src/routes/`:

1. **landing/Landing.jsx** - Home page (composition of multiple sections)
   - Imports: Hero, Partners, Feature, Cta, Stats, Pricing, Testimonials, Team, Comparison, Newsletter
   - Receives: `loggedIn` prop

2. **contact/Contact.jsx** - Contact page with form
   - Imports: ContactForm, CareerCta, ContactFormSection, ContactImg
   - No props required

3. **pricing/Pricing.jsx** - Pricing page
   - Imports: PricingComparisonTable, PricingFaq
   - Receives: `loggedIn` prop

4. **faq/Faq.jsx** - FAQ page
   - Imports: FaqList

5. **blog/Blog.jsx** - Blog listing page
   - Imports: BlogGrid

6. **blog-post/BlogPost.jsx** - Individual blog post
   - Uses route parameter `:name`

7. **about/About.jsx** - About page
   - Imports: AboutOne, AboutTwo, AboutThree

8. **terms/Terms.jsx** - Terms of service
   - Static content

9. **refunds/Refunds.jsx** - Refund policy
   - Static content

10. **privacy/Privacy.jsx** - Privacy policy
    - Static content

11. **jobs/Jobs.jsx** - Jobs listing page
    - Imports: JobsListing

12. **sign-in/SignIn.jsx** - Sign in page
    - Imports: SignInForm
    - Receives: `retrieveDatabase`, `user`, `setLoggedIn` props

13. **sign-up/SignUp.jsx** - Sign up page
    - Imports: SignUpForm
    - Receives: `registerUser` prop

14. **user-profile/Profile.jsx** - User profile (protected route)
    - Receives: `user`, `updateUser`, `deleteUser`, `setLoggedIn`, `setTriggeredLogout` props

15. **NotFound.jsx** - 404 page

### 9. All Reusable UI Components

Located in `/src/components/`:

1. **navigation/Navigation.jsx**
   - Header with logo, navigation links, auth buttons
   - Props: `loggedIn`, `setLoggedIn`, `setTriggeredLogout`
   - Uses Tailwind classes

2. **footer/Footer.jsx**
   - Footer with navigation links and social icons
   - Static content with maps over navigation objects
   - No props

3. **cta/Cta.jsx**
   - Call-to-action section
   - Props: `loggedIn`
   - Link to sign-up or profile based on auth state

4. **team/Team.jsx**
   - Team display component

5. **Newsletter.jsx**
   - Email subscription form
   - Uses state: `formValue`, `formError`, `submit`, `loading`
   - Form validation

6. **JobsForm.jsx**
   - Job application form
   - Uses state for form management

7. **stories/Button.jsx** - Storybook example component
8. **stories/Header.jsx** - Storybook example component
9. **stories/Page.jsx** - Storybook example component

### 10. Package Manager

**Package Manager:** npm (confirmed by presence of `package-lock.json`)

**Lock File:** `/package-lock.json` exists

**Package.json Key Info:**
- `"type": "module"` - ES modules enabled
- `"private": true` - Private package

### 11. TypeScript Config

**No TypeScript config found** in project root. Project uses JSX with JavaScript (.jsx/.js files), not TypeScript.

Storybook uses TypeScript for config files (`main.ts`, `preview.tsx`) but the app code is pure JavaScript/JSX.

### 12. Existing Storybook Config

**Main Config:** `//.storybook/main.ts`
```typescript
- stories pattern: '../src/**/*.mdx', '../src/**/*.stories.@(js|jsx|mjs|ts|tsx)', './eval-support/*.mdx'
- addons: @chromatic-com/storybook, @storybook/addon-vitest, @storybook/addon-a11y, @storybook/addon-docs, @storybook/addon-onboarding
- framework: @storybook/react-vite
```

**Preview Config:** `//.storybook/preview.tsx`
```typescript
- controls matchers for color and date
- a11y addon with test: 'todo'
```

**Vite Integration:** Uses `@storybook/react-vite` as framework

**Testing:** Configured with `@storybook/addon-vitest`, Vitest, and Playwright for browser testing

### 13. Existing Stories

Located in `/src/stories/`:

1. **Button.stories.js**
   - Button component with Primary, Secondary, Large, Small variants
   - Uses `fn()` for click handlers
   - Autodocs enabled

2. **Header.stories.js**
   - Header component with LoggedIn and LoggedOut variants
   - Props: user, onLogin, onLogout, onCreateAccount

3. **Page.stories.js**
   - Page component with LoggedOut and LoggedIn variants
   - LoggedIn variant includes interaction test (play function)

4. **Configure.mdx**
   - Documentation page about configuring Storybook

### 14. CSS Framework

**Tailwind CSS v4 with @tailwindcss/vite plugin**

**CSS Setup:**
- `/src/core-ui/index.css` - Main CSS file
  - Imports `@import "tailwindcss";`
  - Defines CSS custom properties:
    - `--primary-font-color: rgb(147, 157, 250)` (purple)
    - `--primary-font-color-hover: rgb(82, 98, 245)` (darker purple)
  - Base styles for body and code

**Tailwind Config:** `/tailwind.config.js`
```javascript
content: ["./index.html", "./src/**/*.{js,jsx,ts,tsx}"]
theme: { extend: {} }
plugins: []
```

**No CSS modules or styled-components used** - Pure Tailwind + custom CSS variables

**CSS Classes Example:**
- `bg-gray-900`, `text-white`, `flex`, `items-center`, etc.

### 15. Router

**Router:** react-router-dom v7.7.0

**Type:** BrowserRouter with declarative Routes

**Configuration:**
- No basename specified
- Standard React Router setup with `<Routes>` and `<Route>` components
- Conditional routing based on `loggedIn` state for protected routes
- useNavigate hook used for programmatic navigation
- Link components throughout for navigation

**Navigation Features:**
- Reset scroll on navigation: `window.scrollTo(0, 0)`
- Route parameters supported: `/blog/:name`
- Catch-all wildcard route for 404s: `<Route path="*" element={<NotFound />} />`

---

## Summary

This is a **Next.js/React marketing and SaaS dashboard website** for "Edgy" - an Instagram growth agency. Key characteristics:

- **No Context API or Provider pattern** - Simple prop drilling for auth state
- **Minimal data fetching** - Basic fetch() calls to Airtable backend
- **SessionStorage-based auth** - Session management via browser storage
- **Tailwind CSS + custom CSS variables** - Styling approach
- **react-router-dom v7** - Client-side routing
- **Vite + React** - Build and dev tooling
- **Storybook 0.0.0 (PR build)** - Component documentation
- **Airtable for database** - Backend is custom Node/Express server with Airtable integration
- **No portals** - Simple DOM structure
- **62 JS/JSX files total** - Modest codebase size
- **ReCAPTCHA integration** - Form verification on sign-up and sign-in

All state management is prop-based, authentication is sessionStorage-based, and there are no complex provider trees or custom hooks for data fetching.

     5→        href: "/blog/how-to-start-an-instagram-blog",
     6→        description: "Starting an Instagram blog is relatively easy and can be done in a few simple steps and some creativity as well",
     7→        date: 'Mar 16, 2023',
     8→        datetime: "2023-03-16",
     9→        imageUrl: "https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1600096194534-95cf5ece04cf?ixlib=rb-1.2.1&ixid=MnwxMjA3fDB8MHxwaG90by1wYWdlfHx8fGVufDB8fHx8&auto=format&fit=crop&w=776&q=80",
    10→        readingTime: '5 min',

    12→            name: 'Roel Aufderehar',
    13→            imageUrl: 'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1472099645785-5658abf4ff4e?ixlib=rb-1.2.1&ixid=eyJhcHBfaWQiOjEyMDd9&auto=format&fit=facearea&facepad=2&w=256&h=256&q=80',
    14→        },
    15→        blogPost: `In today's digital age, social media has become an integral part of our lives, and Instagram, with its visually captivating platform, stands out as a popular choice for content creators looking to start a blog. Whether you're passionate about fashion, travel, food, fitness, or any other niche, Instagram offers an excellent opportunity to showcase your creativity and engage with a global audience. So, if you're eager to share your passions with the world, here's a step-by-step guide on how to start an Instagram blog and make it a resounding success.\n\n1. Define Your Niche:\nBefore diving headfirst into Instagram blogging, it's crucial to identify your niche. Determine what interests you the most and where your expertise lies. A well-defined niche helps you stand out in a sea of content creators and attracts a dedicated audience genuinely interested in your content.\n\n2. Create a Compelling Instagram Handle:\nYour Instagram handle is essentially your brand name, so make it catchy and reflective of your niche. Keep it simple, memorable, and easy to spell to ensure that potential followers can find and remember you easily.\n\n3. Develop a Cohesive Aesthetic:\nInstagram is a visual platform, and maintaining a consistent and attractive aesthetic is key to grabbing attention and retaining followers. Choose a color palette, editing style, and overall theme that aligns with your niche and reflects your personality.\n\n4. Craft an Engaging Bio:\nYour Instagram bio is your chance to make a great first impression. Use it to concisely describe who you are, what your blog is about, and what value you provide to your followers. Include relevant keywords and a clear call-to-action (CTA) to encourage visitors to follow you.\n\n5. Share High-Quality Content:\nQuality is paramount on Instagram. Invest in a good camera or use your smartphone's advanced camera features to capture stunning images. Ensure your photos are well-lit, well-composed, and visually appealing. Additionally, use engaging captions that provide context, ask questions, or encourage interaction.\n\n6. Plan Your Content Strategy:\nConsistency is key to Instagram success. Plan your content in advance using content calendars or scheduling tools. This will help you maintain a steady flow of posts and ensure that you cover a diverse range of topics within your niche.\n\n7. Utilize Instagram Stories and IGTV:\nInstagram Stories and IGTV are powerful tools for connecting with your audience in a more authentic and engaging way. Use Stories to share behind-the-scenes content, polls, Q&A sessions, and announcements. IGTV allows for longer-form content, giving you an opportunity to showcase in-depth tutorials, travel vlogs, or interviews.\n\n8. Engage with Your Audience:\nInteract with your followers regularly by responding to comments, answering direct messages, and acknowledging user-generated content (UGC). Building a strong relationship with your audience fosters loyalty and encourages them to support your blog in the long run.\n\n9. Collaborate and Network:\nCollaborations with other content creators, brands, or influencers can significantly boost your exposure. Look for like-minded individuals or companies within your niche and explore partnership opportunities that align with your brand values.\n\n10. Monitor Analytics and Adapt:\nKeep a close eye on Instagram Insights to analyze the performance of your posts, identify the best times to post, and understand your audience demographics. Use this data to adapt your content strategy and optimize your Instagram blog for better engagement and growth.\n\nStarting an Instagram blog may seem overwhelming, but with passion, dedication, and a well-thought-out approach, success is within reach. Remember, it's not just about followers and likes; it's about creating meaningful connections and sharing your unique voice with the world. So, take the leap, stay true to yourself, and watch your Instagram blog thrive!`

    20→        href: '/blog/what-to-post-on-instagram',

    22→            'There are many different types of content that you can post on Instagram to engage with your followers and grow your following.',
    23→        date: 'Mar 10, 2023',
    24→        datetime: '2023-03-10',
    25→        imageUrl:
    26→            'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1611162616305-c69b3fa7fbe0?ixlib=rb-1.2.1&ixid=MnwxMjA3fDB8MHxwaG90by1wYWdlfHx8fGVufDB8fHx8&auto=format&fit=crop&w=1674&q=80',
    27→        readingTime: '4 min',
    28→        author: {
    29→            name: 'Brenna Goyette',
    30→            imageUrl:
    31→                'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1550525811-e5869dd03032?ixlib=rb-1.2.1&ixid=eyJhcHBfaWQiOjEyMDd9&auto=format&fit=facearea&facepad=2&w=256&h=256&q=80',
    32→        },
    33→        blogPost: `
    34→Instagram is a powerful platform for engaging with your audience, but knowing what to post can sometimes be challenging. To help you keep your feed interesting and your followers engaged, we've put together a list of content ideas that you can use to make your Instagram shine!\n\n
    35→1. Behind-the-Scenes:\n
    36→Take your followers behind the scenes and show them the process of creating your products or services. People love to see the human side of your brand and get a glimpse of what goes on behind closed doors.\n\n
    37→2. User-Generated Content (UGC):\n
    38→Encourage your followers to create content featuring your products or services and share it on their own profiles. UGC not only gives your brand credibility but also helps foster a sense of community among your followers.\n\n
    39→3. Inspirational Quotes:\n
    40→Share motivational and inspirational quotes that align with your brand values. Quotes are highly shareable and can resonate with your audience on a personal level.\n\n
    41→4. Product or Service Highlights:\n
    42→Highlight your best-selling products or most popular services. Showcase their features and benefits to entice your audience to make a purchase.\n\n
    43→5. Tutorials and How-Tos:\n
    44→Educate your followers with step-by-step tutorials and how-to guides related to your industry. Providing value through educational content can establish your brand as an authority in your niche.\n\n
    45→6. Customer Testimonials:\n
    46→Share positive feedback and testimonials from satisfied customers. Testimonials build trust and social proof, which can encourage others to do business with you.\n\n
    47→7. Polls and Surveys:\n
    48→Engage your audience by running polls and surveys in your Instagram Stories. This not only encourages interaction but also helps you gather valuable insights from your followers.\n\n
    49→8. Throwback Posts:\n
    50→Take a trip down memory lane and share old photos or milestones from your brand's history. Nostalgic content can evoke positive emotions and create a sense of nostalgia among your audience.\n\n
    51→9. Seasonal Content:\n
    52→Tailor your content to fit holidays, seasons, or special occasions. Seasonal posts show that your brand is current and relevant.\n\n
    53→10. Fun Facts and Trivia:\n
    54→Share interesting and fun facts related to your industry or niche. Fun and quirky content can entertain your audience and keep them coming back for more.\n\n
    55→11. Collaborations and Partnerships:\n
    56→Highlight any collaborations or partnerships you have with other brands or influencers. Collaborative content can introduce your brand to a new audience and increase your reach.\n\n
    57→12. Company Culture:\n
    58→Give your followers a glimpse into your company culture and values. Share team photos, company events, and charitable initiatives.\n\n
    59→Remember, the key to a successful Instagram feed is to mix up your content and keep it engaging. Experiment with different types of posts and see what resonates best with your audience. Happy posting!`

    69→        imageUrl:
    70→            'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1505322747495-6afdd3b70760?ixlib=rb-1.2.1&ixid=MnwxMjA3fDB8MHxwaG90by1wYWdlfHx8fGVufDB8fHx8&auto=format&fit=crop&w=1770&q=80',

    76→        },
    77→        blogPost: `Instagram Creator Studio is a powerful tool that allows creators and businesses to manage their Instagram content and account from a desktop computer. If you want to optimize your Instagram presence and streamline your content creation process, here's everything you need to know about Instagram Creator Studio:\n\n
    78→1. Overview of Features:\n
    79→Instagram Creator Studio offers a range of features to help you manage and analyze your content. You can schedule and publish posts, view post insights, manage comments and direct messages, and track audience demographics.\n\n
    80→2. Content Scheduling:\n
    81→One of the key benefits of Instagram Creator Studio is the ability to schedule your posts in advance. This feature allows you to plan and organize your content calendar, ensuring a consistent posting schedule.\n\n
    82→3. Insights and Analytics:\n
    83→Understanding your audience and post performance is crucial for Instagram success. Instagram Creator Studio provides detailed insights and analytics, allowing you to track the reach, engagement, and impressions of your posts.\n\n
    84→4. Post Management:\n
    85→Easily manage your published posts through Instagram Creator Studio. You can edit captions, add location tags, and even delete or archive posts directly from your desktop.\n\n
    86→5. Content Library:\n
    87→The content library feature enables you to organize your media, making it easier to access and reuse your photos and videos for future posts.\n\n
    88→6. Direct Messaging:\n
    89→Stay connected with your followers by managing your Instagram direct messages through Creator Studio. Respond to messages and engage with your audience efficiently.\n\n
    90→7. Monetization Features:\n
    91→For eligible creators, Instagram Creator Studio provides access to monetization features such as IGTV ads and brand partnerships. This opens up opportunities for generating revenue through your Instagram content.\n\n
    92→8. Video Publishing:\n
    93→Creator Studio also supports video publishing, making it convenient to upload, schedule, and manage your IGTV videos directly from your desktop.\n\n
    94→9. Cross-Platform Management:\n
    95→In addition to Instagram, Creator Studio also allows you to manage content for your Facebook Pages. This integrated platform simplifies content management across both social media platforms.\n\n
    96→10. Accessible for Instagram Business and Creator Accounts:\n
    97→Instagram Creator Studio is available for both business accounts and creator accounts. If you have an Instagram business or creator account, you can access Creator Studio for free.\n\n
    98→In conclusion, Instagram Creator Studio is a valuable tool for Instagram content creators and businesses alike. Its range of features and user-friendly interface make it a must-have for anyone looking to enhance their Instagram presence. Take advantage of Instagram Creator Studio's capabilities and elevate your Instagram strategy to new heights!`

   107→        date: 'Mar 16, 2023',
   108→        datetime: '2023-03-16',
   109→        imageUrl:
   110→            'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1539187577537-e54cf54ae2f8?ixlib=rb-1.2.1&ixid=MnwxMjA3fDB8MHxwaG90by1wYWdlfHx8fGVufDB8fHx8&auto=format&fit=crop&w=776&q=80',
   111→        readingTime: '5 min',

   116→        },
   117→        blogPost: `Looking to up your Instagram game and boost your engagement? Here are 20 tactful Instagram tips to help you create a compelling and successful presence on the platform:\n\n
   118→1. Define Your Audience:\n
   119→Understand your target audience and create content that resonates with their interests and preferences.\n\n
   120→2. Showcase Your Personality:\n
   121→Let your personality shine through your posts and stories to connect with your followers on a personal level.\n\n
   122→3. Use High-Quality Images:\n
   123→Invest in high-quality photography or use professional editing apps to enhance the visual appeal of your posts.\n\n
   124→4. Craft Engaging Captions:\n
   125→Write captivating captions that complement your images and encourage your followers to interact with your content.\n\n
   126→5. Leverage Hashtags:\n
   127→Use relevant and trending hashtags to expand your reach and attract new followers.\n\n
   128→6. Engage with Your Audience:\n
   129→Respond to comments, direct messages, and mentions to foster a sense of community and build meaningful relationships.\n\n
   130→7. Collaborate with Others:\n
   131→Team up with other creators or brands for collaborations and cross-promotion to reach new audiences.\n\n
   132→8. Utilize Instagram Stories:\n
   133→Take advantage of Stories to share behind-the-scenes content, polls, Q&A sessions, and daily updates.\n\n
   134→9. Post Consistently:\n
   135→Maintain a consistent posting schedule to keep your audience engaged and coming back for more.\n\n
   136→10. Analyze Your Insights:\n
   137→Regularly check your Instagram Insights to understand what content performs best and tailor your strategy accordingly.\n\n
   138→11. Showcase User-Generated Content (UGC):\n
   139→Feature UGC from your followers to foster a sense of community and encourage others to create content related to your brand.\n\n
   140→12. Host Giveaways and Contests:\n
   141→Organize fun giveaways and contests to incentivize engagement and attract new followers.\n\n
   142→13. Share Behind-the-Scenes:\n
   143→Offer a glimpse into your creative process or daily life to make your content more relatable and authentic.\n\n
   144→14. Use Instagram Reels and IGTV:\n
   145→Explore Instagram Reels and IGTV for creating entertaining and longer-form content to diversify your feed.\n\n
   146→15. Optimize Your Bio:\n
   147→Craft a concise and impactful bio that clearly defines your brand and what you offer to your followers.\n\n
   148→16. Monitor Your Competitors:\n
   149→Keep an eye on your competitors' strategies to stay informed about industry trends and stay ahead.\n\n
   150→17. Follow Instagram Trends:\n
   151→Stay updated on the latest Instagram features and trends to keep your content fresh and relevant.\n\n
   152→18. Use Instagram Shopping:\n
   153→Leverage Instagram Shopping to tag products in your posts and stories for a seamless shopping experience.\n\n
   154→19. Track Link Clicks:\n
   155→Use link tracking tools to monitor the performance of links in your bio or swipe-up links in Stories.\n\n
   156→20. Stay Authentic and Transparent:\n
   157→Be true to yourself and your brand values. Authenticity builds trust and loyalty among your followers.\n\n
   158→By implementing these tactful Instagram tips, you can elevate your Instagram presence, foster meaningful connections, and create a vibrant and engaged community of followers. Happy Instagramming!`

   176→        },
   177→        blogPost: `If you're a business looking to harness the power of Instagram for marketing, you're in the right place. Here are some essential Instagram marketing resources to help you build a strong presence and achieve your business goals:\n\n
   178→1. Business Account Features:\n
   179→Switch to a business account to access valuable features like Instagram Insights, contact information, and the ability to run ads.\n\n
   180→2. Instagram Ads Manager:\n
   181→Use the Ads Manager to create and manage Instagram ad campaigns that target your desired audience.\n\n
   182→3. Instagram Shopping:\n
   183→Set up Instagram Shopping to tag products in your posts and enable a seamless shopping experience for your followers.\n\n
   184→4. IGTV for Business:\n
   185→Leverage IGTV to showcase long-form content, behind-the-scenes videos, and product demonstrations.\n\n
   186→5. Instagram Stories Ads:\n
   187→Create engaging Stories ads to reach your audience in a creative and interactive way.\n\n
   188→6. Instagram Hashtag Strategy:\n
   189→Develop a hashtag strategy to increase your reach and visibility to a relevant audience.\n\n
   190→7. Influencer Marketing:\n
   191→Collaborate with influencers in your niche to extend your brand's reach and credibility.\n\n
   192→8. Instagram Content Calendar:\n
   193→Plan and schedule your Instagram content in advance using a content calendar to maintain consistency.\n\n
   194→9. User-Generated Content (UGC):\n
   195→Encourage your followers to create and share content related to your brand to foster authenticity and community.\n\n
   196→10. Instagram Insights:\n
   197→Analyze your Instagram Insights to understand your audience, track performance, and refine your strategy.\n\n
   198→11. Instagram Stories Highlights:\n
   199→Curate and organize your Stories Highlights to showcase your best content and products.\n\n
   200→12. Instagram Analytics Tools:\n
   201→Use third-party analytics tools to gain deeper insights into your Instagram performance.\n\n
   202→13. Instagram Contests and Giveaways:\n
   203→Run contests and giveaways to engage your audience and drive excitement around your brand.\n\n
   204→14. Instagram Live:\n
   205→Go live on Instagram to connect with your followers in real-time and host Q&A sessions or product launches.\n\n
   206→15. Instagram Insights API:\n
   207→Leverage the Insights API to access more in-depth data and integrate it into your existing analytics tools.\n\n
   208→16. Instagram Reels for Business:\n
   209→Create entertaining and creative Reels to capture your audience's attention and showcase your products.\n\n
   210→17. Instagram Guides:\n
   211→Use Guides to create valuable and informative content that provides value to your audience.\n\n
   212→18. Instagram Engagement Strategies:\n
   213→Develop strategies to encourage engagement, such as asking questions, running polls, and responding to comments.\n\n
   214→19. Instagram Algorithm Understanding:\n
   215→Stay informed about the Instagram algorithm and adjust your content strategy accordingly.\n\n
   216→20. Instagram for Business Blog:\n
   217→Stay up to date with the latest Instagram updates and best practices through the official Instagram for Business Blog.\n\n
   218→By utilizing these Instagram marketing resources, you can optimize your business's presence on the platform, engage your audience, and achieve your marketing objectives effectively.`

   233→                'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1472099645785-5658abf4ff4e?ixlib=rb-1.2.1&ixid=eyJhcHBfaWQiOjEyMDd9&auto=format&fit=facearea&facepad=2&w=256&h=256&q=80',
   234→        }, blogPost: `Running an Instagram contest can be an exciting way to engage your audience and boost brand awareness. Here's a step-by-step guide on how to run a successful Instagram contest:\n\n
   235→        1. Define Your Goals:\n
   236→        Determine the purpose of your contest. Are you looking to increase followers, generate user-generated content (UGC), or promote a new product?\n\n
   237→        2. Choose the Contest Type:\n
   238→        Decide on the type of contest that aligns with your goals. Popular options include like-to-win, photo contests, and hashtag challenges.\n\n
   239→        3. Set Clear Rules and Guidelines:\n
   240→        Establish straightforward and easy-to-understand rules for participation, eligibility, and prize distribution.\n\n
   241→        4. Select a Relevant Prize:\n
   242→        Offer a prize that resonates with your target audience and encourages them to participate.\n\n
   243→        5. Create Eye-Catching Graphics:\n
   244→        Design visually appealing graphics and promotional materials to announce the contest.\n\n
   245→        6. Decide on the Contest Duration:\n
   246→        Choose a specific start and end date for the contest to create a sense of urgency.\n\n
   247→        7. Promote the Contest:\n
   248→        Use your social media channels, email list, and website to promote the contest and reach a wider audience.\n\n
   249→        8. Monitor Entries and Engage:\n
   250→        Keep track of contest entries, interact with participants, and respond to questions or concerns.\n\n
   251→        9. Choose a Fair Winner:\n
   252→        Select the contest winner fairly and transparently based on the stated rules.\n\n
   253→        10. Announce the Winner:\n
   254→        Publicly announce the winner and thank all participants for their involvement.\n\n
   255→        11. Follow Legal and Instagram Guidelines:\n
   256→        Ensure your contest complies with all relevant laws and Instagram's promotion guidelines.\n\n
   257→        12. Measure Results:\n
   258→        Analyze the success of the contest based on your initial goals and use the insights to improve future contests.\n\n
   259→        13. Repurpose UGC:\n
   260→        Leverage the user-generated content from the contest to showcase your brand and products.\n\n
   261→        14. Express Gratitude:\n
   262→        Show appreciation to your audience for participating and supporting your brand.\n\n
   263→        Running an Instagram contest can not only drive engagement but also foster a sense of community around your brand. By following these steps, you can create a fun and rewarding experience for your followers and achieve your marketing objectives.`
   264→
   265→    },

   281→        },
   282→        blogPost: `Gaining followers on Instagram is a common goal for many users and businesses. If you're looking to increase your follower count, here are some effective strategies to help you grow your Instagram following:\n\n

   286→Share visually appealing and engaging content that resonates with your target audience.\n\n

   289→4. Collaborate with Influencers:\n

   339→        },
   340→        blogPost: `Promoting your eCommerce store on Instagram can significantly boost your online presence and drive sales. If you're looking to leverage Instagram to grow your eCommerce business, here are some effective strategies to get started:\n\n
   341→1. Optimize Your Instagram Profile:\n
   342→Ensure your Instagram bio clearly communicates your brand, products, and value proposition. Use a recognizable profile picture and include a link to your eCommerce store.\n\n
   343→2. Create High-Quality Visuals:\n
   344→Post eye-catching product photos and lifestyle images that showcase your products in the best light. Use professional photography or high-quality graphics to make your content stand out.\n\n
   345→3. Use Product Tags and Shoppable Posts:\n
   346→Take advantage of Instagram's product tags and shoppable posts features to make it easy for users to shop directly from your posts.\n\n
   347→4. Run Instagram Ads:\n
   348→Invest in Instagram ads to reach a wider audience and promote your best-selling products or special offers.\n\n
   349→5. Host Giveaways and Contests:\n
   350→Organize giveaways and contests to create buzz around your eCommerce store and attract new followers and potential customers.\n\n
   351→6. Collaborate with Influencers:\n
   352→Partner with influencers in your niche to showcase your products to their engaged audience and gain credibility.\n\n
   353→7. Utilize Instagram Stories and Highlights:\n
   354→Use Instagram Stories to share behind-the-scenes content, product demonstrations, and limited-time offers. Save important Stories as Highlights on your profile for easy access.\n\n
   355→8. Offer Exclusive Promotions:\n
   356→Provide exclusive discounts or promotions to your Instagram followers to incentivize them to make a purchase.\n\n
   357→9. Engage with Your Audience:\n
   358→Respond to comments, direct messages, and engage with your followers to build a loyal customer base.\n\n
   359→10. Collaborate with Other Brands:\n
   360→Partner with complementary brands for co-marketing initiatives or joint promotions to expand your reach.\n\n
   361→11. Showcase User-Generated Content (UGC):\n
   362→Repost and feature user-generated content that showcases customers using and enjoying your products.\n\n
   363→12. Leverage Instagram Guides and Reels:\n
   364→Create helpful product guides or entertaining Reels that highlight your products and brand personality.\n\n
   365→13. Utilize Instagram Shopping Features:\n
   366→Make use of Instagram Shopping features like the Shop tab on your profile and the Shopping Explore page to increase product visibility.\n\n
   367→14. Promote Seasonal and Holiday Offers:\n
   368→Plan and promote seasonal sales and holiday offers to capitalize on festive shopping trends.\n\n
   369→15. Monitor and Analyze Performance:\n
   370→Regularly review Instagram Insights and track the performance of your posts and campaigns to refine your strategies.\n\n
   371→16. Showcase Customer Reviews and Testimonials:\n
   372→Feature positive customer reviews and testimonials to build trust and social proof.\n\n
   373→17. Use Instagram Live:\n

   379→20. Measure ROI and Adjust Strategies:\n
   380→Continuously measure the ROI of your Instagram efforts and adjust your promotional strategies based on the data to optimize results.\n\n
   381→By implementing these Instagram marketing strategies, you can effectively promote your eCommerce store and drive meaningful engagement and conversions.`

   384→        title: 'A Beginner’s Guide to Instagram for Businesses',
   385→        href: '/blog/how-to-use-instagram-for-business-a-beginners-guide/',
   386→
   387→        description:
   388→            'Instagram for business is a way for companies to promote their products or services on the popular social media platform.',
   389→        date: 'Mar 16, 2023',
   390→        datetime: '2023-03-16',
   391→        imageUrl:
   392→            'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1598128558393-70ff21433be0?ixlib=rb-1.2.1&ixid=MnwxMjA3fDB8MHxwaG90by1wYWdlfHx8fGVufDB8fHx8&auto=format&fit=crop&w=1089&q=80',
   393→        readingTime: '5 min',
   394→        author: {
   395→            name: 'Daniela Metz',
   396→            imageUrl:
   397→                'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1487412720507-e7ab37603c6f?ixlib=rb-1.2.1&ixid=eyJhcHBfaWQiOjEyMDd9&auto=format&fit=facearea&facepad=2&w=256&h=256&q=80',
   398→        },
   399→        blogPost: `If you're a business looking to harness the power of Instagram for growth and success, this beginner's guide is for you. Instagram is a valuable platform for businesses to connect with their target audience, build brand awareness, and drive sales. Here's how to get started with Instagram for business:\n\n
   400→1. Set Up an Instagram Business Account:\n
   401→Create a dedicated business account on Instagram. It provides access to valuable insights and features like Instagram Ads and Shopping.\n\n
   402→2. Optimize Your Profile:\n
   403→Use your business name as your username and include a recognizable profile picture, preferably your logo. Write a compelling bio that clearly communicates your brand identity and value proposition.\n\n
   404→3. Define Your Target Audience:\n
   405→Identify your target audience and understand their preferences, interests, and pain points. This knowledge will guide your content strategy.\n\n
   406→4. Create High-Quality Visual Content:\n
   407→Invest in creating visually appealing and high-quality images and videos that represent your brand. Use professional photography and graphic design to make your content stand out.\n\n
   408→5. Develop a Content Strategy:\n
   409→Plan your content in advance to ensure consistency and relevance. Mix promotional content with valuable and entertaining posts to keep your audience engaged.\n\n
   410→6. Use Hashtags Strategically:\n
   411→Research and use relevant hashtags to reach a wider audience and increase discoverability. Create a branded hashtag to encourage user-generated content (UGC).\n\n
   412→7. Engage with Your Audience:\n
   413→Respond to comments, messages, and mentions promptly. Engage with your audience through likes, comments, and reposts of UGC.\n\n
   414→8. Utilize Instagram Stories and Highlights:\n
   415→Leverage Instagram Stories to share behind-the-scenes content, product launches, and limited-time offers. Save important Stories as Highlights on your profile to showcase them permanently.\n\n
   416→9. Collaborate with Influencers and Partners:\n
   417→Partner with influencers and complementary businesses to extend your reach and credibility.\n\n
   418→10. Run Instagram Contests and Giveaways:\n
   419→Organize contests and giveaways to boost engagement and attract new followers.\n\n
   420→11. Measure Performance with Instagram Insights:\n
   421→Regularly review Instagram Insights to understand your audience demographics, post reach, and engagement. Use these insights to refine your strategies.\n\n
   422→12. Utilize Instagram Ads:\n
   423→Invest in Instagram Ads to reach a broader audience and promote your products or services effectively.\n\n
   424→13. Monitor Your Competitors:\n
   425→Keep an eye on your competitors' Instagram activities to gain insights into what works and what doesn't in your industry.\n\n
   426→14. Leverage Instagram Shopping Features:\n
   427→Use Instagram Shopping to tag products in your posts and Stories, making it easy for users to shop directly from your content.\n\n
   428→15. Showcase Customer Testimonials:\n
   429→Feature positive customer reviews and testimonials to build trust and credibility.\n\n
   430→16. Analyze and Adjust Your Strategy:\n
   431→Regularly analyze the performance of your Instagram efforts and adjust your strategy based on the results.\n\n
   432→By following these tips and best practices, you'll be well on your way to establishing a strong presence on Instagram and driving business growth. Remember, consistency and authenticity are key to success on this vibrant social platform.`
   433→    }

content.text:
Perfect! I've successfully retrieved all the requested files. Here's a comprehensive report of the full contents:

## File Contents Report

### 1. src/components/navigation/Navigation.jsx
Navigation component for the Edgy website with responsive header and navigation links. Includes authentication state management with Sign In/Sign Up buttons when not logged in, and Sign Out/Profile when logged in. Navigation links include: About, Pricing, Blog, FAQ, Contact.

### 2. src/components/footer/Footer.jsx
Footer component with multiple sections: Solutions (contact info), Support (Pricing, FAQ, Contact), Company (About, Blog, Jobs), Legal (Terms, Privacy, Refunds). Includes social media links to GitHub, Portfolio, and LinkedIn. Dynamic copyright year using JavaScript.

### 3. src/components/cta/Cta.jsx
Call-to-action component with "Ready to dive in?" messaging and "Get started" button. Button routes to /sign-up for non-logged-in users or /profile for logged-in users.

### 4. src/components/Newsletter.jsx
Newsletter subscription form component with email validation using regex pattern. Features loading state with animated spinner, success message, and error handling. Privacy policy link included.

### 5. src/components/NotFound.jsx
404 Not Found page component with featured Giphy image. Displays helpful navigation links to popular pages: Pricing, Blog, Jobs, FAQ. Includes back-to-home link.

### 6. src/components/team/Team.jsx
Team display component that renders team members from data/team.js. Shows team member profiles with images, names, roles, and social links (Twitter and LinkedIn).

### 7. src/routes/landing/Landing.jsx
Main landing page composition that includes: Hero, Partners, Feature, Cta, Stats, Pricing, Testimonials, Team, Comparison, and Newsletter components. Note: Line 18 has a typo with "Cta g" instead of just "Cta".

### 8. src/routes/faq/Faq.jsx
FAQ page displaying frequently asked questions with FaqList component and CTA section.

### 9. src/routes/about/About.jsx
About page composition with three about sections (AboutOne, AboutTwo, AboutThree), Team component, and CTA.

### 10. src/routes/pricing/Pricing.jsx
Pricing page displaying pricing information with comparison table (PricingComparisonTable), pricing FAQ, and partners section.

### 11. src/routes/blog/Blog.jsx
Blog page with blog grid layout and newsletter subscription component.

### 12. src/routes/contact/Contact.jsx
Contact page with ContactImg, ContactFormSection, and CareerCta components.

### 13. src/routes/jobs/Jobs.jsx
Jobs page displaying job listings and jobs application form.

### 14. vite.config.js
Vite configuration with:
- React and Tailwind CSS plugins
- Vitest testing setup with Storybook addon
- Playwright browser testing configuration
- Mobile device simulation options (iPhone 17)
- CSS minification and code splitting enabled

### 15. src/data/ Files

**blogPostsData.js** - 10 blog posts about Instagram growth and marketing strategies with titles, dates, authors, reading times, and full post content.

**companyInformation.js** - Company stats: Founded 2022, 200 Employees, 521 Beta Users, $25M Raised

**companyStats.js** - 4 key metrics: 5K+ Happy Customers, 205M+ Followers Gained, +150% Increased Engagement, +33% Increased Leads

**faq.js** - 8 FAQ items covering: How Edgy works, follower expectations, industry requirements, follower importance, manual work, getting started, free trial, post-signup process

**features.js** - 6 features: Flexible plans, Targeted audience, Content management, Organic followers, Advanced security, 24/7 support

**jobs.js** - 4 job openings: HR Assistant, Growth Specialist, Senior React Developer, Junior Customer Support

**partners.js** - 5 partner companies: AirBNB, Duolingo, FinalCut, Fiverr, Figma (with linked logos)

**pricingFaq.js** - 6 FAQ items specific to pricing (subset of main FAQ)

**pricingSections.js** - Pricing structure with Features, Reporting, and Support sections organized by three tiers: Standard, Premium, Business

**pricingTiers.js** - Three pricing plans:
- Standard: $79/month
- Premium: $149/month
- Business: $349/month

**team.js** - 6 team members with names, roles, images, and social media links (all using placeholder URLs)
